title: Protecting democracy in Europe: pluralism, autocracy and the future of the EU [Book Review]
author: Schupmann

opening_move.description: The review opens by noting the EU's complicity in democratic backsliding — through membership fatalism and depoliticized responses — and presents Theuns's book as an ambitious normative-institutional account of how the EU can legitimately defend democracy in member states.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: The EU's response to democratic erosion must go beyond procedural containment to treat antidemocrats as genuine political threats — but this requires resolving a tension between substantive democratic values and the means of defending them

theoretical_positioning.description: Substantive critical review endorsing Theuns's project while identifying an internal tension: the book defends substantive democratic values but constrains the means of their defence in ways that may be insufficient. The reviewer is broadly sympathetic but raises this as a genuine problem.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: policy analysis; legal analysis of EU institutional mechanisms
data_sources: EU Treaty on European Union (Article 2, Article 7)
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: normative political theory; institutional analysis of EU mechanisms
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review (4 pages). The EU institutional mechanisms (Article 2, 7; infringement proceedings; financial conditionality) are used as illustrative cases within a normative argument about democratic defence.

case_type.case_name: Theuns, Protecting Democracy in Europe (2024); EU institutional mechanisms

confidence_notes: Book review (4 pages). The reviewer's identification of an internal tension in Theuns's argument gives this more critical depth than a standard expository review. The EU institutional material is illustrative of normative claims.
